items.passage: Plants make their own food through a process called photosynthesis. They take in sunlight through their green leaves and use it to power tiny chemical reactions. Water comes up from the roots and carbon dioxide enters through small holes on the leaves. The plant turns these into sugar that helps it grow and gives off oxygen as waste. Most of the oxygen in the air comes from this simple process every day.

items.passage: Some plants and animals that move to new places can cause serious problems for the local environment. These newcomers are called invasive species, and they often spread very quickly without natural enemies. Without predators to keep them in check, they can take over and harm the native wildlife. Scientists try to control invasive species by removing them or stopping new arrivals at the border.

items.passage: People often think they make decisions based only on careful and balanced thinking. However, our minds use mental shortcuts that can lead to common errors called cognitive biases. For example, we tend to remember things that match what we already believe and forget the rest. These shortcuts help us think quickly but they can also make us miss important information.

items.passage: Long before modern instruments, sailors found their way across the open ocean by watching the stars. They used the position of the sun by day and the pole star by night. Some sailors also studied wind patterns, ocean waves, and the flight paths of seabirds. Later, the magnetic compass arrived from China and made sea travel safer and more accurate. These early skills helped traders cross great distances and connect cultures around the world.

items.passage: Erosion is the slow process that breaks down rocks and moves soil from one place to another. Rain, rivers, wind, and ocean waves all cause erosion over many years. Sharp mountain peaks become rounded as small pieces of stone wash away with the water. Strong winds carry sand that wears down rocks in dry places like deserts. Without plants holding the soil in place, erosion can quickly damage farmland and rivers.

items.passage: Between Mars and Jupiter lies a wide region of space filled with millions of rocky bodies. These small objects are called asteroids, and together they form what we know as the asteroid belt. Most asteroids are too small to be planets but they share the same path around the sun. Some scientists believe these rocks are leftover material from the early days of our solar system. Studying them may help us learn how the planets formed billions of years ago.
